facade_colour: dark red-brown brick with white painted window frames and trim

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular-style House-form building that reflects architectural styles of the period. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties retain many of their original building features including a shared roofline, dormer windows, mirrored facades, second storey bay windows, segmentally arched openings with brick voussoirs, and brick cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_description: 147 Denison Ave presents a house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include original windows, bay window, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as an early-twentieth-century vernacular-style House-form building that reflects architectural styles of the period. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, the properties retain many of their original building features including a shared roofline, dormer windows, mirrored facades, second storey bay windows, segmentally arched openings with brick voussoirs, and brick cladding. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
